input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system studied is the serial industrial robot MABI MAX 100. It is a six‑axis robot where each axis is driven by a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor (the first axis is explicitly described). The robot is controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. It operates in manufacturing/automation environments for tasks such as precise positioning, assembly, or material handling, and its modular mechanical structure and open controller make it a suitable candidate for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin supplies active software components that interpret the one‑way data flow from the physical object, enabling processing of contextual data traces, decision support, and the potential for closed‑loop control or optimization functions.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) tooling: SysML (including IBDs and state‑machine diagrams), architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc (with MontiArc Automaton for behavior), simulation environments like MathWorks Simulink, and development in general‑purpose languages (C++, Java). These tools are used to model hierarchy, interfaces, behavior, and to generate executable code for the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows the Digital Shadow Reference Model, consisting of Asset, Model, DataTrace, Processing, and Source entities. A Processing component consumes input DataTraces, applies engineering or simulation models, and emits output DataTraces that become part of the shadow. Associations such as 'originates from' provide transitive traceability from output traces back to source components and models. This component‑&‑connector style orchestration integrates heterogeneous models (e.g., SysML, simulation tools) and manages resources through explicit data‑flow links, embodying a model‑driven integration pattern.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin aggregates multiple computational models: structural/geometric models captured in SysML block diagrams and internal block diagrams; behavioral models expressed as SysML state machines or automata; analytical/physics‑based models implemented in MathWorks Simulink for dynamic simulation; and data‑driven (black‑box) models such as machine‑learning regressors. Model definitions originate from engineering artifacts (requirements, functional and logical architecture) and are transformed through traceable model‑to‑model pipelines (e.g., SysML → Simulink). Data sources are the contextual data traces collected from the physical system (digital shadow) and are managed by aggregation and abstraction mechanisms defined in the digital‑shadow reference UML class model. Relationships between models are maintained via component‑connector links in ADLs and via explicit mapping artefacts, enabling the Twin to synchronize model state with incoming data.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by combining white‑box (physics‑based Simulink) and black‑box (data‑driven) models, as recommended for accurate friction identification. The approach stresses traceability of data to model elements and verification of model transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models) to guarantee correctness. Uncertainty arising from model approximations and data preprocessing is acknowledged, and quality assurance relies on explicit traceability links and verification steps described in the literature.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The digital shadow implements an automated one‑way data flow from the physical object to its digital representation. It continuously collects contextual data traces from the physical system, aggregates and abstracts them for a specific purpose, and stores them as a digital object. The flow is passive; the digital shadow does not send data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: A Digital Twin can provide active components that enable bidirectional communication, allowing the digital model to push data or commands back to the physical system. The documents mention that a digital twin may contain active components for control, but they do not describe command types, control message formats, validation/authentication mechanisms, feedback systems, conflict resolution, or command execution confirmation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between physical devices and the digital twin relies on the MQTT protocol, with sensors acting as MQTT clients publishing time‑series data to an MQTT broker (e.g., Mosquitto). The network follows a client‑broker topology over IP networks. The documents reference the use of an IoT platform (ThingsBoard) that can ingest MQTT streams, implying a layered architecture where MQTT feeds into the platform’s data processing modules. No explicit bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security (e.g., TLS) specifications are provided in the sources.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The deployment environment for the digital shadow/Digital Twin includes cloud‑based IoT platforms such as ThingsBoard and may incorporate Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized representation, as mentioned in the literature. Visualization is performed through a dashboard (e.g., Angular‑based front‑end). While the texts refer to cloud‑based technologies at the machine shop‑floor level, concrete details on compute/storage sizing, scalability mechanisms, deployment models (cloud, edge, on‑premise), or availability assurances are not provided.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a highly detailed and specific description of the physical system, accurately identifying the robot model (MABI MAX 100), its six-axis structure, the permanent-magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. It also correctly identifies the operational domain (manufacturing/automation) and suitability for digital twinning. This is strongly supported by the source text: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' (Section III), 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor' (Section III), and 'robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' (Section III).

The extraction accurately reflects the paper's definition of the Digital Twin's role relative to the Digital Shadow, specifically providing active components to process data for decision support and control improvement. Supported by quotes: 'software systems require active components to interpret and process this incoming data', 'A Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components', 'This data should then be applied to improve the robot control’s accuracy'.

The extraction provides a comprehensive and highly specific list of the tools, languages, and environments mentioned in the background section, correctly associating them with their technical roles (modeling hierarchy, behavior, simulation). Supported by quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc or SysML IBDs', 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7', 'MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'real-time' data and 'live measurements', and refers to data as 'time series'. However, it does not specify synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs between the physical and digital components as defined by the characteristic. The lack of these specific technical details justifies the 'Not in Document' classification.

The source documents focus exclusively on a single digital shadow for the 'first axis of the MABI robot'. There is no mention of multiple digital twin instances, centralized versus decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between different twins. Thus, the 'Not in Document' statement is accurate.

The extraction accurately describes the system's adherence to the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model', correctly listing the entities (Asset, Model, DataTrace, Processing, Source). It accurately details the role of the Processing component ('consumes input DataTraces... produces output data'), the 'originates from' association for 'transitive traceability', and the 'component & connector' perspective used for integration. It also correctly identifies the use of heterogeneous models like 'SysML' and 'simulation tools'. The description is highly specific and well-grounded in the provided source.

The source documents mention specific technologies like Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT, but they do not describe integration with external systems, other Digital Twins, or enterprise systems as defined by the characteristic. The scope is limited to the internal connection between the robot and its digital shadow. Consequently, the 'Not in Document' claim is correct.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately synthesizes the system's modeling approach, citing SysML for structure ('Block Definition Diagram', 'Internal Block Diagram') and Simulink for analytical/physics-based simulation. It captures the mix of white-box and black-box models and correctly identifies the Digital Shadow Reference Model for data aggregation. However, it includes unsupported specific details, such as 'machine-learning regressors' (text only says 'data-driven') and 'traceable model-to-model pipelines (e.g., SysML → Simulink)' (text mentions using both, but not a transformation pipeline).

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text provides detailed information about the physical actuators. Specifically, Section III describes the 'permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', and 'cycloidal gear'. It also mentions control via the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' and limitations such as the 'maximum working range' and 'maximum achievable speed'. Therefore, the extraction is incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the sensing infrastructure. Section III mentions 'two position encoders per drive', specifically 'The motor encoder' and 'secondary encoder'. Section IV further details data access: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT. These are the input current and the output angle.' Thus, the extraction is incorrect.

The extraction correctly identifies the hybrid modeling approach for accuracy ('combine white- and black-box friction models') and the reliance on traceability for quality assurance. It accurately reflects the methodology where simulated data is compared against measured data ('deviation of simulated data versus measured data') to validate the models. The description is specific to the system's approach to handling uncertainty and model verification.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the digital shadow as a passive, one-way flow from physical to digital, which is strongly supported by the source text. It correctly identifies the concepts of data traces, aggregation, and purpose. Quotes: 'A digital shadow is characterized by “an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object”' and 'Therefore, a digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.' The extraction is detailed but focuses on the general definition rather than specific implementation protocols like MQTT found later in the text.

The extraction accurately reflects the source text which states that a digital shadow 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system' and that 'A Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components'. The extraction correctly notes that the documents mention these active components but do not provide specific details on command types or formats, as the text focuses on the passive digital shadow aspect.

The extraction correctly identifies MQTT as the protocol, but it includes unsupported specific details not found in the source, such as the use of Mosquitto, ThingsBoard, client-broker topology, and IP networks. The source only mentions that sensors are 'streamed via MQTT'. The mention of specific broker and platform software constitutes reliance on outside knowledge.

The extraction correctly mentions Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and a dashboard, which are in the source. However, it incorrectly asserts the use of specific technologies—ThingsBoard, Angular, and cloud-based platforms—which are not mentioned in the provided source text. This violates the instruction to use only provided evidence. Quotes: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard'.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
